title: Smoothed computational complexity of pure Nash equilibria in general congestion games
background_latex:
A (finite) congestion game consists of:
1) A set of players \(N=[n]\).
2) A set of resources \(R\).
3) For each player \(i\in N\), a strategy set \(\Sigma_i\subseteq 2^R\) (each strategy is a subset of resources).
4) For each resource \(r\in R\), a latency (cost) function \(\kappa_r:[n]\to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\).

For a strategy profile \(\sigma=(\sigma_1,\dots,\sigma_n)\in \Sigma:=\Sigma_1\times\cdots\times\Sigma_n\), the load on resource \(r\) is
\[\ell_r(\sigma):=|\{i\in N: r\in \sigma_i\}|.\]
The cost of player \(i\) under \(\sigma\) is
\[C_i(\sigma):=\sum_{r\in \sigma_i} \kappa_r(\ell_r(\sigma)).\]
A pure Nash equilibrium (PNE) is a profile \(\sigma^*\in \Sigma\) such that for all \(i\in N\) and all deviations \(\sigma_i'\in \Sigma_i\),
\[C_i(\sigma^*)\le C_i(\sigma_i',\sigma^*_{-i}).\]

Under the smoothed-analysis model used in the paper, numerical input parameters are random and independent with densities bounded by \(\phi\). For example:
1) General (explicit) latencies: each \(\kappa_r(\ell)\) is drawn independently from a distribution on \([0,1]\) with density at most \(\phi\).
2) Polynomial latencies: each coefficient \(\alpha_{r,j}\) in \(\kappa_r(\ell)=\sum_{j=0}^{d_r} \alpha_{r,j}\ell^j\) is drawn independently from a distribution on \([0,1]\) with density at most \(\phi\).
3) Step-function latencies: each jump \(\alpha_{r,j}\) at a fixed breakpoint is drawn independently from a distribution on \([0,1]\) with density at most \(\phi\).

A natural algorithm for computing a PNE is better-response dynamics (or best-response dynamics in network congestion games): repeatedly let a player change strategy to strictly decrease her cost. Convergence is guaranteed because congestion games are exact potential games (Rosenthal potential). The paper proves polynomial smoothed convergence time for certain restricted classes (e.g., \(B\)-restrained games where every unilateral deviation changes at most \(B\) resources; and certain compact network congestion games).
problem_definition_latex: Determine whether there exists an algorithm that, given as input an arbitrary (not restricted to special subclasses) \(n\)-player congestion game instance under one of the smoothed perturbation models above (with independent densities bounded by \(\phi\)), computes an exact pure Nash equilibrium in time polynomial in the input size and \(\phi\) (in expectation over the perturbations). Equivalently, resolve the smoothed computational complexity of finding an exact PNE in general congestion games outside the restricted instances for which polynomial smoothed-time convergence is currently known.
latex:
A (finite) congestion game consists of:
1) A set of players \(N=[n]\).
2) A set of resources \(R\).
3) For each player \(i\in N\), a strategy set \(\Sigma_i\subseteq 2^R\) (each strategy is a subset of resources).
4) For each resource \(r\in R\), a latency (cost) function \(\kappa_r:[n]\to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\).

For a strategy profile \(\sigma=(\sigma_1,\dots,\sigma_n)\in \Sigma:=\Sigma_1\times\cdots\times\Sigma_n\), the load on resource \(r\) is
\[\ell_r(\sigma):=|\{i\in N: r\in \sigma_i\}|.\]
The cost of player \(i\) under \(\sigma\) is
\[C_i(\sigma):=\sum_{r\in \sigma_i} \kappa_r(\ell_r(\sigma)).\]
A pure Nash equilibrium (PNE) is a profile \(\sigma^*\in \Sigma\) such that for all \(i\in N\) and all deviations \(\sigma_i'\in \Sigma_i\),
\[C_i(\sigma^*)\le C_i(\sigma_i',\sigma^*_{-i}).\]

Under the smoothed-analysis model used in the paper, numerical input parameters are random and independent with densities bounded by \(\phi\). For example:
1) General (explicit) latencies: each \(\kappa_r(\ell)\) is drawn independently from a distribution on \([0,1]\) with density at most \(\phi\).
2) Polynomial latencies: each coefficient \(\alpha_{r,j}\) in \(\kappa_r(\ell)=\sum_{j=0}^{d_r} \alpha_{r,j}\ell^j\) is drawn independently from a distribution on \([0,1]\) with density at most \(\phi\).
3) Step-function latencies: each jump \(\alpha_{r,j}\) at a fixed breakpoint is drawn independently from a distribution on \([0,1]\) with density at most \(\phi\).

A natural algorithm for computing a PNE is better-response dynamics (or best-response dynamics in network congestion games): repeatedly let a player change strategy to strictly decrease her cost. Convergence is guaranteed because congestion games are exact potential games (Rosenthal potential). The paper proves polynomial smoothed convergence time for certain restricted classes (e.g., \(B\)-restrained games where every unilateral deviation changes at most \(B\) resources; and certain compact network congestion games).

Determine whether there exists an algorithm that, given as input an arbitrary (not restricted to special subclasses) \(n\)-player congestion game instance under one of the smoothed perturbation models above (with independent densities bounded by \(\phi\)), computes an exact pure Nash equilibrium in time polynomial in the input size and \(\phi\) (in expectation over the perturbations). Equivalently, resolve the smoothed computational complexity of finding an exact PNE in general congestion games outside the restricted instances for which polynomial smoothed-time convergence is currently known.
open_problem_quote: However, outside of these restricted instances of the problem, the complexity remains unresolved.
subject_classification: Smoothed analysis of algorithms for game-theoretic local search (PLS / congestion games)
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement asks to resolve smoothed polynomial-time solvability for broad classes of congestion games; there is no concrete finite parameter range or explicit conjectured bound to falsify by searching small instances, and separating polynomial vs superpolynomial smoothed complexity is not amenable to brute-force counterexample discovery.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Automatically evaluating candidate algorithms would require reliably estimating expected convergence time over continuous perturbations and proving polynomial bounds across all instances, which is not an automated fitness signal. The problem is primarily proof-theoretic rather than search-and-evaluate.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
